The material that you've learned in this course is powerfully influential to your negotiation arsenal. It gives you the ability to subtly open your counterparts' perception to see your side. There is a wide range of uses for these techniques, from negotiating the release of a hostage to closing a huge merger or contract deal. And of course, we also apply these skills directly to negotiating great salaries. Here are three counterintuitive, but crucial steps. Each step is a blend of the dynamics and skills you've learned in the previous chapters. They will not only bring you more money, but also arm your boss to fight on your behalf. The first step is to be pleasantly persistent on non-salary terms. What pleasant persistence does is create an emotional framework that promotes empathy with your boss and fosters a collaborative environment for productive dialogue. In any negotiation, we are simultaneously auditioning our skills for how we will be ambassadors for those we are associated with. Everyone wants a representative who will champion their interests in a way that not only doesn't damage relationships, but encourages them. In this context, the less you focus on the actual money and talk about non-salary items, the more likely you are to hear the full range of options. And if they are unable or unwilling to meet your non-salary request, they may just counter with more money. Examples of non-salary terms you might ask for include additional vacation days, print a leave, or other company benefits. Second, before you have settled on an acceptable salary offer, define what success for your position would look like, as well as measurable metrics that show the value of your contributions. This shows not only that you care about the position, it shows that you care about the organization as a whole. This doesn't cost your boss a single penny, clearly lays out the groundwork for future raises, and is a constant reminder to yourself and your boss of your value. Third, spark your boss' interest in your success, not just in the position, but in the company, and gain an unofficial mentor at the same time. A great question to ask is, what does it take to be successful here? Not only does it show that you want to be successful and that you care about the job, it also makes your boss feel more involved in your development because they are giving you guidance. They will have a personal stake in seeing you succeed. They are also, therefore, going to watch and see if you follow their advice. If you do what they advise and don't succeed, then the failure is theirs as well. So actually following what they've said is the necessary element for them to be willing to clear the path for you. Whether they know it or not, you will have just recruited your first unofficial mentor. You're essentially bending their reality to include you as part of their success.
